title: 25 Words About My Left Shoe

title: Jazzwood, chapter 3
subtitle: Part 2
content:
Genre: Fiction
Previously: Chapter 1, Chapter 2 part 1 and part 2, Chapter 3 part 1
Note: I appreciate your patience with the funky posting schedule the last couple weeks. We’re adjusting to a new school year in our house, and I’m looking to switch my weekly deadline from Tuesdays to Fridays (which I typically have off of work). Regardless, thanks for reading and subscribing! And happy Labor Day! Enjoy. :)

A skinny guy with a dirty blonde man bun whose name I can’t remember stands up and walks toward the door. 

“Whoa! Alex, right?” he says, ushering me toward the group. “Carrie’s brother?”

“Y–yeah,” I manage, and then, because I’m predisposed to awkwardness and have no earthly idea what to say next, “How are you guys?”

Most if not all of the you guys in question had shown up at Carrie’s funeral a week and a half ago. All sorts of folks came out of the woodwork that day—a muddled blur of handshakes and disembodied faces offering condolences and paying respects. I hadn’t made the connection at the time, but it seems likely that the group circled up in front of me now were Carrie’s addict friends. 

“Good, man. We’re good. I’m Brandon. How are you? Are you here to join us tonight, or..?”

His question dangles in the dank basement air, and I can feel the expectant gaze of seven or eight pairs of eyes on me as I calculate my response. 

“Sure. Yeah. I mean, if that’s allowed?”

Brandon’s sandpapery laugh breaks the tension. “Everyone’s welcome here, man. Take a seat.”

The meeting plays out with little fanfare and (I must say) a disappointing dearth of helpful information. It’s a fun cast of characters, to be sure, and I can definitely see why Carrie would have appreciated the camaraderie, but as far as I can tell there’s no “JW” here. There’s Brett, a gray-haired Army-looking guy with neck and face tattoos and a surprisingly gentle voice; Luther, a giant, avuncular man missing all four fingers on both hands; and Stacy, a middle-aged woman in a sleeveless R.E.M. t-shirt with a pronounced smoker’s cough. Not one name that begins with J, though.

The group methodically makes its way around the circle, each person offering a life update of sorts. More mundanity that I’d expected, honestly. Lots of “one day at a time”s. As Stacy shares her story, I’m reminded that at the funeral she introduced herself to me as Carrie’s sponsor. I make a note to connect with her after the meeting—maybe she has information that would provide useful.

When the floor’s offered to me, I fumble my way through an explanation of my unusual presence, something about being there to observe and my gratitude for their role in Carrie’s life. Empathetic head nods and a chorus of “Thanks, Alex!” wraps up my meager portion of the meeting. Soon enough, everyone’s standing up, holding hands, and jointly reciting the Serenity Prayer with an earnest reverence I find rather touching:

God, grant us the serenity to accept the things we cannot change,
the courage to change the things we can,
and the wisdom to know the difference.

Never been much of a pray-er myself, but hey, I’ll accept all the serenity, courage, and wisdom I can get right now, supernaturally imparted or not. The group lazily disperses; both Brett and Luther pat me on the back on their way out—a final offering of sympathy, I assume. The only ones left now other than me are Brandon and Stacy, who are returning the last of the chairs to a rusty rack. 

“Thanks again for having me,” I say. “I really appreciate the hospitality.”

“Of course, man, of course,” Brandon says, tucking a strand of hair behind his ear. “Glad to have you. Your sister was one-of-a-kind.”

I nod. “Yeah, she was.”

“I gotta run, though, man. I’m sure we’ll run into each other again… I totally dig that store of yours. Either way, hang in there. And see you next week, Stace!” 

Stacy’s halfway to the door herself, perforating the silence with the staccato slaps of a fresh pack of cigarettes against her palm. She turns back toward me, the look on her face saying You gonna join me, or what? 

Out in the stairwell, the air is humid, but mercifully absent the smell of mildew. Stacy lights up and offers me her lighter, which I accept.

“You were Carrie’s sponsor?” I phrase it like a question even though I know the answer, to break the ice.

Stacy nods and exhales a cloud of smoke. “Since her very first meeting, yeah. Still weird not to hear from her every morning, you know?” She grimaces. “Shit, of course you know.”

“No, it’s okay. I know what you mean. So was this, like, her regular group? I could’ve sworn you guys met at a Presbyterian church.”

“Westminster, yeah. That is where we usually meet, but they’re closed for a few months for remodeling, so we relocated here temporarily. Actually, our first meeting here at First was the Sunday before…” She trails off, neither wanting nor needing to finish the sentence. “Anyway, between you and me, honey, once you’ve seen one church basement, you’ve seen ‘em all.” 

I take a long drag before responding, in part to consolidate my churning thoughts. The note on Carrie’s calendar—the note that led me here in the first place—was in all likelihood entered as a reminder of her NA meeting’s location change. That doesn’t explain the JW, though. 

“This may be a weird question, but I noticed that Carrie had written down the meeting you mentioned—the first one here—in her calendar, along with the letters J.W. Any idea what that might m—”

Before I can finish, Stacy keels over in gravelly laughter, which almost instantly morphs into an lengthy coughing fit. She holds up a hand (I’m okay!) and nods her head between convulsions (Yes, I know what that means!). At last, the hacking subsides.

“Junkies Weekly!” she says, clearing her throat. “That was her nickname for our group. She meant it as a term of endearment, though. Even junkies gotta have a sense of humor… Hell, your sister sure did.”

Somehow I am both deflated and buoyed by this revelation. Junkies Weekly is a perfectly clever Carrie-ism, even though it gets me precisely nowhere closer to finding Jazzwood. 

Above us, the yellow-tinted lights in the church parking lot flicker on—it’s getting dark. Stacy flicks what’s left of her cigarette into the bucket.

“So but she was there that night?” I ask.

She starts walking up the stairs. “Yep. Only thing out of the ordinary—if that’s what you’re getting at—is she called me on Wednesday or Thursday of that week saying she had the urge to use again real bad, which was unusual for her. ‘Stress’ was all I could coax out of her for what it was that was triggering her, but we worked through the moment.” A pause. “I felt like we did.”

“No indication she’d been using that week,” I reassure her, “so whatever you said or did must’ve worked. Thank you… For taking her under your wing.”  

“Honey, it was an absolute honor sponsoring your sister,” she says. “You know, this might not be my place to say, but addiction and grief have more in common than you’d probably think. People think addiction’s something you can ‘break’ or something. Gotta kick my bad habit, that type of thing. But every addict who’s actually acknowledged it knows that you can’t break an addiction. Not happenin’. Once an addict, always an addict. It’s just whether you’re in recovery or not.

“Anyway, point is, after you’ve been sad for a while, everyone’s all like ‘Get on with it,’ you know? But grief’s not about getting on with it, it’s about getting on with it. It ain’t ever going away, honey. And the sooner you learn that, the better.”  

And then suddenly Stacy’s pulled me into a firm embrace. Up close, she smells like Pall Malls and perfume. The impromptu bit of intimacy, however graceless, releases a gratifying fraction of the tension in my body. 

We separate, and she opens the door of her Jeep—the only vehicle remaining in the church lot. “You need a ride somewhere?”

“Nah, I’m good. Thank you, though. For everything.”

Stacy smiles. “You take care of yourself now, honey.”

Her Jeep putters away, leaving me alone in the parking lot, steeped in the golden glow of the aging streetlights.

As I near Center Street on my return walk, I decide to stop for a drink or two at C–Bock’s to decompress. I could head home and squeeze in a few hours of video game streaming, but, well, it’s been a day. Chris notices me as soon as I’m in the door. As always, he’s tending the bar, polishing glasses with flair and laying the charm on thick as he intermittently refills drinks for the handful of regulars and guests. There’s one of those new internet-connected jukeboxes on the wall back by the pool table—Shania’s lamenting That don’t impress me much over the familiar din of the room.

Without having to ask, Chris pours me a cold Blue Moon and sets it on a purple Scribbles ’N Bits coaster in front of me—one of our many cross-promotional marketing schemes hatched over the years—as I plop onto a stool. The beer is crisp and astonishingly refreshing.

“Didn’t expect to see you again tonight,” Chris says. “Aren’t Sundays your regular streaming night?”

“Usually, yeah. Not feeling it, I suppose. This beer’s hitting the spot, though, I can tell you that. Thanks.”

“You bet. Any big plans for your day off tomorrow?”

“Not really, no.” I’m unsure how much information to divulge just yet. It’s not that I don’t trust him; it’s more that I barely have a handle on the situation myself. What would I even say at this point? Ultimately I opt for a highly-redacted version of the truth: “I have a therapy appointment, but that’s about it.”

“Well, say hi to the doc for me.”

Someone at the end of the counter signals Chris for another round. I absentmindedly absorb a few sports highlights from the TV mounted behind the bar when my phone vibrates with a notification that instantly snaps me into full consciousness:


After a half-second’s pause, I chug what’s left of the beer, slap a twenty on the counter, and exit the bar at nearly full speed—like, cartoon-smoke-cloud-in-my-wake speed—toward my car in the alley.

Looks like I’ll be streaming tonight after all. 

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

title: Jazzwood, chapter 3
subtitle: Part 1
content:
Genre: Fiction
Previously: Chapter 1, Chapter 2 part 1 and part 2

My name is Carrie and I’m an addict.

I’ve never actually been to Narcotics Anonymous, but I imagine that’s what Carrie would say every time she went to one of her meetings. Sitting in some dimly lit room on an ancient folding chair, drinking burnt coffee and commiserating over shared impulses—I’ve seen enough NA and AA meetings in movies and TV shows to pretty much get the gist, I think. 

That’s gotta be what this calendar entry is. Except I’m pretty sure her weekly meeting was at a Presbyterian church. No, it definitely was. So what was she doing at this other church, then? Meeting up with JW, apparently. I mouth it slowly: Jay double-you… 

Jazzwood.

I pull up First Lutheran’s surprisingly not-terrible website on my phone and navigate to their online calendar, where today’s schedule is highlighted:

SUNDAY

8:30am–9:30am: Worship (Sanctuary)
9:30am–10:00am: Fellowship (Fellowship Hall)
1:00pm–3:00pm: Faith Knitters (Library)
8:00pm–9:00pm: Narcotics Anonymous (Fellowship Hall)

To be sure it’s a weekly occurrence, a few taps backwards in time takes me to the week from Carrie’s calendar. And there it is. So she went to this other NA meeting six days before she died, and it was an important enough that she wrote it down. Maybe JW/Jazzwood is some sort of street name, like a drug dealer or undercover cop or something? Or maybe I’ve seen too many movies and TV shows. Regardless, I need to get to the bottom of this, and a glance at the time—7:42 according to my phone—all but makes the decision for me: I’m going to my first NA meeting tonight.

First Lutheran is only a few blocks away from the store. Walking distance, in other words. Emma closed down the front of the store, so I hit the office lights and head out into the alley, locking the door behind me. The sun’s nearly set, but the air still weeps with wet heat, like a washcloth that’s still damp even after it’s been wrung out. I emerge on the deserted sidewalk and hang a left in the direction of the church. I check the time: 7:44. Should make it just in time.

Because the store’s closed on Mondays, on Sunday nights I usually head straight home and dive right into a marathon late-night streaming session. My video game speedrunning side-hustle brings in a couple hundred bucks each month, thanks to a small but mightily devoted (and generous!) group of subscribers… but tonight they’ll have to wait. I suppose it depends on what—if anything—I’m able to learn at this meeting. STBDWL for sure.

What’s important right now, as I make my way there, is to come up with a game plan. What exactly am I hoping to learn at this meeting? The obvious answer is who “JW” is, but a few other questions come to mind. I take out my pack of cigarettes and make a mental list as I light up: The meeting was written in in her appointment book, but did Carrie actually attend? And will anyone there tonight know? If JW was there, what business did Carrie have with him or her? I’m fairly certain she wouldn’t’ve been looking to score; for one thing, I doubt she’d be so careless as to leave a physical record of a rendezvous with a dope slinger. But more practically, if she’d started using again it would’ve shown up in her toxicology report, which was cleaner than the lyrics on a KIDZ BOP album.

That drugs weren’t involved in her death had been a relief, to be honest. A strangely uplifting gift in the midst of the nearly crippling weight of it all. Like, Yay, it wasn’t an overdose! Fucking worthless consolation, in hindsight. As I round the corner at a four-way stop, the weathered limestone exterior of First Lutheran comes into view, about three-quarters of a block down. Almost there.

We were pretty transparent with one another, but the entire subject of Carrie’s sobriety was kind of an interpersonal Bermuda Triangle—the handful of times it’d been broached, conversations would just straight up evaporate, so I’d kind of decided it was best to just avoid it altogether. Adjust course, I guess, and navigate around the issue to more favorable waters. She was dedicated to her sobriety, that much was obvious. All’s I’m saying is that her willingness or desire to talk about it was like, nonexistent. With me, anyway.

Actually, the one exception I can remember is when Carrie justified her participation in NA to me, deploying another water-based metaphor: She said she’d used opioids a bit recreationally—Percocet, maybe?—and likened it to splashing around in the shallow end of a pool, in the kiddie section. Good times, right? But she’d gotten scared she would lose her bearings, that she’d drift inexorably toward the deep end and before she knew it wind up literally in over her head. By then it’d be too late, and concepts like “fun” or “recreational” would be vapor, if anything at all. She went to NA, she said, to keep reminding herself of the importance of staying out of the pool in the first place. And as far as I know, she never did get back in.

A sign on the church’s front doors displays the stylized NA logo (which has always reminded me of the iconic black-on-yellow Batman symbol) and an arrow, indicating I should enter in a side door, presumably. There doesn’t appear to be anyone outside, but as I walk around the side of the building I notice a handful of cars in the church parking lot. A set of cement stairs leads to a door on the lower level. Another NA sign. I stick what’s left of my cigarette into a sand-filled 5-gallon bucket, alongside hundreds of others. A miniature cemetery diorama made of cigarette butts. The permissible vice, I suppose. Hopefully this door leads directly to the Fellowship Room or whatever it’s called and not a musty maze of hallways and mystical church paraphernalia. 

Door open, fluorescent light. Good news: No maze. And this is definitely a Narcotics Anonymous meeting. Problem is, there’s nothing anonymous about it. I recognize all of these people.

And they recognize me.

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

title: Mêtà-tête
subtitle: The peril and promise of overthinking small talk
content:
Genre: Nonfiction / personal essay
Note: This essay was sent originally to paid subscribers three months ago; I’m re-posting it for free on the occasion of receiving the tattoo (!) discussed below. This newsletter exists to develop my skills as a writer, and paid subscribers receive new writing across a variety of genres weekly. Consider subscribing!

When I received my first vaccine shot in March, a single tear trailed cinematically down my cheek as the needle bit into my arm1—a tiny, physical manifestation of the eddy of emotions swirling inside in that climactic moment. Okay, not really. The emotions were real, yes, but I didn’t actually shed any tears. What actually happened is that after I sat down, I was feeling rather emotional, and so I was also thinking about the fact that I was feeling emotional, which prompted me to ask the woman administering the shots, “Have you had anyone get emotional? Like, tearing up or anything?” To which she replied, “No, not really... Mostly people are just happy.” Totally, I nodded. Weird, I thought. Feels like a significant moment, though, doesn’t it? Yes, definitely. Better explain yourself. “It just feels like a significant moment, is all,” I said. I wondered if others really were emotional and just didn’t show it or if I was the weirdo, then weighed whether I should verbalize that thought and decided against it. “Thank you,” I said. The whole scene played out in maybe 30 seconds. 

That exchange is, for me, SOP. In fact, this kind of social interaction—the actual verbal communication along with my internal monologue—happens so frequently that I’ve come to think of it as a defining characteristic of both my psyche and my social self. I am still processing the following conversation with a physician assistant from a routine medical appointment a couple of weeks ago, for example (note that I was carrying a copy of Andy Weir’s new novel, Project Hail Mary):

PA: [Gesturing] “If you could just step up on the scale here… What’re we reading?”

Me: “What are we reading…” Does she really want to know? Maybe she’s a reader. But also, maybe she’s not? Does it matter? I suppose an easy thing would be to say “Oh, just a fun sci-fi book,” because it’s true, plus it would as they say put the ball back in her court AND have the added benefit of giving her the option to follow up if she’s truly interested, BUT… it’s by Andy Weir, and he wrote The Martian—which she’s almost certainly heard of, given that it was adapted into a blockbuster film starring Matt Damon. How much time has passed since she asked? How much what do they call it in broadcasting? Oh yeah, dead air! How much dead air is acceptable, anyway? Would any response at this point imperil the whole exchange? Nah, probably it’s fine… The latter option’s more interesting, go with that. “Oh, it’s the new book by Andy Weir… he’s the guy who wrote—

PA: [Interrupting] “Cool, you can go ahead and head into this room here…”

Me: “Cool.”

That is not an exaggeration. Doesn’t matter when or where they happen—these seemingly benign tête-à-têtes render me suspendedly animated in the moment and then interpersonally Monday morning quarterbacking later on, sometimes for months.2 Doctor’s office? Check. Grocery store checkout? Yup. Impromptu backyard chat with the new neighbor? You bet your ass. Even with friends and family. Maybe even especially with friends and family. Anyway, I’ve been thinking about this a lot lately, trying to make sense of the whole business, and what I keep coming back to is… marbles.

There’s a viral video from a few years back that depicts a “marble machine” separating a huge collection of marbles via a (supposedly) random, Plinko-like process into inexplicably homogeneous color groupings:3

Now imagine you’re a physician assistant, say. Every week, you see tens or hundreds of patients, represented in this thought experiment by colored marbles. Each patient/marble is a real person, a unique snowflake, a complex and beautiful narrative incarnate. And although there are many paths your conversations with a patient/marble may take, they’ll inevitably wind up landing in one of like seven possible slots: The guy who talked about the rain they’re predicting for later this week (plink, plink, blue); the kid who was a bit of a pain to wrangle through the intake process (plink, plink, orange); the woman who comments on your appearance (plink, plink, green). And so on.

Or imagine you’re a barista at a coffee shop. Or a defense attorney. Or a nurse volunteering to administer coronavirus vaccines. Plink, plink. Our personal and professional lives are littered with uncomplicated conversations that slot neatly into predestined containers. Unless I’m an insane person, you can probably conjure an example or two yourself.

And listen, I totally get it. Probably the majority of folks are just looking to make it to dinnertime having made small talk that was easy both to give and to receive. Formulaic, like a flowchart. In and out. I’m not disparaging this impulse, either. Way back in the 1960s, etiquette and cookbook author Peg Bracken said that the foundation of good manners is “doing those things that let one remain at ease, of never putting another person on the spot, and of not making others uncomfortable.” And she’s right. Coming to understand the mechanics of small talk and developing relative proficiency at it benefits the Other as much as oneself, and probably more.

But what it boils down to is I don’t want to be any of the marble colors. I’m not interested in a conventional interaction that’s instantly forgettable save for its minuscule contribution to a data set of carbon-copy conversations. I want to be literally outside of the box, a new color defined by its inquisitiveness about the other colors. This impulse isn’t about me; it’s not that I want to be perceived as interesting or engaged or whatever. It’s about you. And the problem is that you are so much more—inconceivably more—than small talk could ever even remotely convey.

If all goes according to plan, in the coming months I’ll be getting a second long-awaited, custom Paul Soupiset tattoo on my forearm. Here’s the artwork:

The design4 is a (masterfully) rendered visualization of a passage from one of David Foster Wallace’s short stories with which I’ve long felt a deep connection:

“You already know the difference between the size and speed of everything that flashes through you and the tiny inadequate bit of it all you can ever let anyone know. As though inside you is this enormous room full of what seems like everything in the whole universe at one time or another and yet the only parts that get out have to somehow squeeze out through one of those tiny keyholes you see under the knob in older doors. As if we are all trying to see each other through these tiny keyholes.”

— David Foster Wallace, Good Old Neon

There’s a whole universe back there! And we both know it even if for whatever reasons we pretend not to. What I’m getting at is: I want to tap into that. If we’re going to small talk, let’s have interesting small talk. By which I mean specifically let’s have a conversation about the conversation, about all the analogous conversations and what they reveal and how they make you feel. Let’s give each other a glimpse of the vast and varied galaxies behind the door. Show me yours and I’ll show you mine.

Meta-conversation is my go-to move, I think, because it’s the etiquette-based, “let one remain at ease” path to a more personal, less formulaic exchange; everyone has the capacity to talk about what they always talk about. But not everyone has the desire to talk about what they always talk about, maybe.

Maybe that’s the disconnect. What I’m trying to say is, This is an opportunity for us to have a deeper, more human interaction, however brief it may be, and the other person can’t or won’t go there, so instead what they say is “You can go ahead and head into this room here.” ✪

1

I elected to go non-dominant, FWIW.

2

It’s possible I’ll remember and dissect the “What’re we reading” physician assistant exchange for years, now that it’s digitally catalogued and (therefore) enduringly referenceable.

3

Couple of things: 1) The video is fake, as proved definitively by the Debunk King of YouTube himself, Captain Disillusion (srsly, smash that subscribe button), but that doesn’t alter the point I’m ultimately attempting to make, I don’t think. 2) The marble machine is modeled on a real-life device called a Galton board, invented in the nineteenth century by Sir Francis Galton to “demonstrate how a normal distribution is formed through the occurrence of multiple random events.” Unlike the colored marble video, what Galton boards actually do is visually produce an approximation of bell-shaped curve distribution. You can even snag yourself a working desk version for a mere $34.95.

4

ICYMI at the beginning of the post, here’s what the final product looks like.

title: A Want to Remember
content:
Genre: Nonfiction / personal essay

Many years ago I read a book about improving one’s memory. Or perhaps improving one’s memory was a small component in a book about something completely different? I guess I don’t remember. Based on the subtitle alone, the obvious candidate is Moonwalking with Einstein: The Art and Science of Remembering Everything,1 but A) I feel like I initially encountered what I’m eventually going to get around to talking about much earlier than Moonwalking, and B) like most sensationalized, focus-grouped subtitles nowadays, Moonwalking isn’t actually about the art or science of remembering everything, but rather (if I remember correctly) a journalist’s quest to compete against other “mental athletes” at the U.S. Memory Championship (which I assure you is a real thing). It’s a decent book, but there’s a marked difference between, say, learning how to efficiently memorize five hundred digits of pi and consistently recalling your kid’s social security number. So it probably wasn’t Moonwalking with Einstein. Leaving us 159 words into an essay in which I’ve already gotten us way off track.2

Anyway, the illustration I’m attempting to conjure for you is a particular piece of advice or in the parlance of our times a “pro-tip” for remembering (maybe more specifically not forgetting), for example, where you’ve put your phone. The pro-tip is as follows:

  • While you’re setting your phone down somewhere (we’ll use the dining room table in this example) say out loud “I am setting my phone on the dining room table.” Don’t whisper it, say it.

  • Thus your brain will have available as rememberable not only the physical act of setting the phone down (which let’s just all agree is borderline autonomic and therefore extremely forgettable, hence the necessitation of this pro-tip in the first place), but also—and this is key—hearing the phrase “I am setting my phone on the dining room table.”3

Voilà. It’s a simple solution to a common problem, and when I remember to do it, it’s certainly effective. Better yet, it requires nothing external—a simple incantation obviates the need for an app, accessory, or any other auxiliary assistance.

Seriously, try it sometime. Now, if you want—I’ve gotta go find my phone.

For various reasons, my six-year-old son Grayson hasn’t yet gotten into movies. Like, as a form of media. He’s seen them—enjoyed them, even—but to my recollection not once in a single sitting outside of a movie theater.4 Movies just aren’t his go-to media; animated episodic television (Bluey FTW), puerile YouTube videos, and even podcasts suit him just fine, it seems. But not for lack on trying on my part.

Which is why I decided to show him the trailer for Godzilla vs. Kong when it came out. Maybe some giant mythical monsters will get him fired up and he’ll want to watch the movie with me, I thought. I cued it up on the television. About halfway through the trailer, the boys are having a little tussle on an aircraft carrier when Kong lands an absolutely massive right hook to ‘Zilla’s face:

At that moment, from across the room I heard Grayson say (half to himself), “Ooooh, I wanna remember that for my good daytime dreams.”

This is not a new observation, but it bears recapitulating: Our ability to outsource the creation and storage of our memories vastly surpasses that of our ancestors, and even that of just one generation prior. Twenty years from now, there’s a decent chance Grayson will still “remember” his reaction to eating apple sauce for the first time or riding to preschool in the backseat every morning or his first time riding a bike. Not because his physical memories of these events are so powerful and enduring, but because he’s seen the pictures and gifs and video recordings of them so many times.

I have many, many memories of moments from Grayson’s life that weren’t captured digitally. But my suspicion is that those undocumented moments will eventually be eclipsed or possibly erased by those which have been captured and stored5 for easy repeat viewing. A precious few of the undocumented memories may live on, perhaps taking the form of family lore lifted up in repeat tellings over the years, but they will have beaten the odds.

That’s why, I think, “I wanna remember that for my good daytime dreams” is such a powerful thought technology. There’s something almost subversive in there, in the acknowledgement that intentional memory–setting has value, that resisting the inexorable (if natural) degradation of memories is a worthy cause. A Gadsden flag flown in protest of autobiographical memory decay. Don’t Fade on Me.

Tangentially related funny story: I vividly remember my high school creative writing teacher laying down the following law: NEVER title a story “A _____ to Remember.” Ever. “A Vacation to Remember”? Disgusting. “A Baseball Game to Remember”? Try again, dummy. “A Prom Night to Remember”? Crime against humanity. “A _____ to Remember,” she told us, is both lazy and banal, and you can do better. One year later, the adaptation of Nicholas Sparks’s A Walk to Remember did $47.5 million at the box office.

This past weekend Grayson and I were in the car running an errand, leisurely making our way along a familiar stretch of road. The temperature outside was unseasonably cool despite the sharp mid-afternoon sun, so we had all four windows down. Coldplay’s Higher Power thrummed from the car’s speakers, the two of us moving semiconsciously to the rhythm of the wind and the music. It was, as they say, a vibe. I looked over my shoulder toward Grayson in the back passenger seat: pajama shorts and t-shirt, sun-tanned arms and legs, blonde hair, and the warmest, most earnest smile I might’ve ever seen. A fleeting, ineffable moment that I felt down to my diaphragm. A breaking-in of the transcendent.

I turned around and thought, “I want to remember that for my good daytime dreams.”

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

1

Which I read in 2013.

2

Maybe it was The Memory Book? I don’t have a specific memory of reading it, but it’s a stronger candidate than Moonwalking. Sorry. Identifying the correct book absolutely doesn’t matter, neither objectively nor in the particular context of this essay, but still… it kinda matters.

3

Not sure if you’d include here remembering the physical sensation of speaking the phrase and/or remembering the inevitable sense of resigned infantilization you feel for actually trying this technique. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

4

Memorably, we sat in the front row for his first in-theater movie, Paddington 2. He slept through most of it.

5

And backed-up, of course.

title: Jazzwood, chapter 2
subtitle: Part 2
content:
Genre: Fiction
Previously: Chapter 1, Chapter 2 (Part 1)
Note: Sorry for the delay this week! This post is the second half of Chapter 2—you may find it helpful to re-read the first half before jumping in below. There’s still so much story to tell, but I’d love your feedback on what I’ve written so far! Also, heads up: I’ll be away all of next week; regular posting will resume on August 3.

My stomach burbles a low complaint. I haven’t eaten since before heading to Carrie’s earlier today. Chased my morning coffee and cigarette with a handful of peanut butter M&M’s. I know. Running on some gnarly fumes here. With practiced efficiency, I take out my phone, open a food delivery app, and tap my way to a favorited and reliably filling fast-food order. Still seems like magic, like I’m conjuring calorie-dense care packages from the ether. Estimated time of delivery: fourteen minutes.

I’m reaching to grab the note from my other pocket when the office door opens. Emma silhouetted in the doorway, Hunger Strike by Temple of the Dog spilling in from the front of the store, her lime green-streaked brunette curls corseted around her head by the retro red-white-and-blue headband embroidered with the Scribbles ’N Bits logo. If there’s any sort of “uniform” for the job, the headband’s it. It’s kinda become our signature. I think mine’s in the car.

“Oh! I didn’t realize you were—”

“Hey, Emma. Just, um, grabbing a few things. I’ll be back for real on Tuesday.” We’re closed on Mondays.

“Oh, no problem.”

If she heard the voice a minute or two ago, she’s not acting like it. Although now that I think about it I’m not sure what “acting like it” would actually entail.

“Hey, thanks again for stepping up these last few weeks. I know it was a lot to ask, and I can’t tell you how much I appreciate it. Things’ve just been…”

“Dude, it’s all good, boss. I mean, not all good, but you know what I mean. I’d basically do anything for you guys.” She paused. “It’s been really… different around here. It’ll be nice to have you back. Everyone’s been giving their condolences. There’s even a few cards.”

Lest anyone tell you that majoring in English is a fool’s errand, it occurs to me now that the word condole means literally “to grieve with” or “to grieve together”—an etymology bordering on laughable at the moment, considering how acutely lonely these last few weeks’ve been. And confusing. And.

“Thanks, Emma.”

“Anyway, I’m just about finished closing up,” she says as she dials the combination on the safe and slides the bank bag inside. “See you on Tuesday, boss.”

As soon as she’s out the door, I retrieve the post-it note from my pocket and gingerly place it on the desk. It is at once insubstantial and exceedingly substantive. It feels… evidential. Exhibit A. But still, enigmatic. A quick web search bears little fruit: Googling “Jazzwood” throws up results for a bougie wooden Bluetooth speaker, a Belgian music festival (and its various social media pages), and some rando from London on Twitter with like 400 followers. And because I’m not a total noob, I search DuckDuckGo, too. Same results, though. Not exactly promising leads. Back to the appointment book.

For a person so expressive and colorful, the planner Carrie kept is rather utilitarian and plain. It’s discordant, the difference. But maybe that was the point; the juxtaposition of, say, a pair of rainbow striped knee-length socks and a pragmatic black spiral-bound planner as a diversion tactic, a means to keep you guessing. Carrie hated being labeled one way or another. And she succeeded, for the most part. That she was a massive and unabashed nerd was the one label she embraced.

A knock on the back door nearly empties my bladder involuntarily. I approach and look through the peephole, exhaling the breath I’d apparently been holding when I see the delivery person holding an insulated bag with my dinner inside. Forgot that my orders automatically append a note to deliver to the back of the store.

“Thanks!” I say, taking the food and closing the door. Back at the desk I dive in, even though the restaurant forgot to include ranch dressing. They always forget the damn ranch. Turns out I was hungrier than I thought.

Another knock at the door. My ranch! I pop up and yank the door open with an enthusiastic “Thank you!” But it’s not the missing dressing. It’s Chris.

“You’re welcome?” he says.

“Thought you were someone else, sorry. Not all there, I guess.” I nose-laugh and gesture inside. “Come in if you want.”

“Nah, it’s cool. I saw your car in the alley and just wanted to say hey is all. Good to see you back at the store. It’s just… good to see you, man.”

Chris and I have been friends—best friends, really—since high school. He owns and manages C–Bock’s, a popular bar that was founded by his dad (Charlie Bock) and passed down to him, C–Bock, Jr. We’ve exchanged a few texts the past couple of weeks, but that’s about it. His sister Cameron died a few years ago of a drug overdose—which fact presently grips my chest with the anxiety of uncertainty about what to say, whether to connect the miserable dots of grief between Cameron and Carrie.

“Thanks. I—”

He shushes me with a sssst and the snapping shut of a pantomimed mouth-hand. “Save the speech, bud. Just know that I know what you’re going through, is all. Okay?”

I nod. 

“You back now?” he says. “Like, officially?”

“No, not officially. Will be on Tuesday.”

“Cool cool. Well, you know where to find me.” He looks over my shoulder. “Looks tasty—I’ll let you get back to it. Did they remember your ranch?”

“Sure didn’t.”

“Assholes.”

“See ya. Thanks for stopping by.” He turns to leave, but I stop him: “Hey, Chris—does Jazzwood mean anything to you?”

“Jazzwood?” 

“Yeah.”

“Nothing comes to mind… Why?”

I wave him off. “No reason. We’ll see ya.”

Chris’s silhouette recedes into the shadowed alley, the sun having slumped past the rooftops some time ago. A quick smoke, then back inside to polish off the last of the food. And get back to the mission.

I thumb the cover of Carrie’s planner and fan the pages like a flip book. Not sure what I’m looking for, to be honest. Anything, I suppose. Not sure where to start, either. Maybe the end. I’ll look at the last days and weeks she was alive and work my way backward. A couple of page turns lands me at the time frame in question. Carrie died on a Saturday, which is blank, as are all of the preceding days that week, except for Sunday. And it’s a doozy:

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

title: Jazzwood, chapter 2
subtitle: Part 1
content:
Genre: Fiction
Previously: Chapter 1

No, what I need is a place to think, to try to make sense of all this.

Before I know it, I’m back in my car and heading to the store. I didn’t really even think about what to do next; after finding the note, my brain’s autopilot must’ve taken over, because the last few minutes are a bit of a blur.

Coming to rest at a stoplight, my surroundings come back into focus: outside, it’s just about what I guess you’d call golden hour. Inside the car it’s stuffy and filled with the sound of some NPR talking head reporting on a new study about the ethics of artificial intelligence design. Dad probably would’ve turned it up—AI was his life’s work—but yeah, now’s not the time. Click. Silence, save for the hum of the engine. Much better. Sorry, Dad. 

Cracking the window and reaching for my pack of smokes, I’m vaguely aware of an almost physical heat radiating from my pants pocket. A quick pat, and the muffled crinkle reassures me that the FIND JAZZWOOD note is safely stowed away for now. I’m pretty sure the note was meant for me, but before diving off the deep end and hunting down Jazzwood (whoever—or whatever—that is), I feel like maybe I should substantiate my hunch. Green light. Light the cigarette.

But see, that’s just it. This is more than a hunch: it’s definitely a message from Carrie to me. It’s a short list of folks who would’ve even found the note, let alone have a special attachment to the pen it was so deliberately hidden inside. Why so cryptic, though? Clearly she thinks it’s an important message, so why didn’t Carrie communicate it to me earlier, before she died? Like, in a text or phone call or email or something? Or you know, in fucking person? 

And what’s with the absolute dearth of context or any additional information whatsoever? Okay, now I’m just getting worked up. Calm down, Alex. Just about to the store, anyhow.

“The store” is of course Scribbles ’N Bits, the business Carrie and I co-own. We’d talked since we were kids about someday running a store together, a kind of nerd sanctuary that combined Carrie’s love of comic books and comic book culture with my passion for video games, and our dream became reality a few years back thanks primarily to a lucky real estate find and a startup investment from our dad. The name had been Carrie’s idea.

Seriously, though, the location is the bomb. Scribbles operates out of a charmingly refurbished row house in “old downtown” at the center of a sedate little township that, since its incorporation in the early 1900s, has been subsumed by the cannibalizing suburbanization of the Big City nearby. Twenty years ago, there may as well’ve been tumbleweeds skittering down the pothole-peppered road; today there are brand-new half-million-dollar houses three miles away, and Center Street is bright, bustling, and lined with tastefully inoffensive landscaping. The potholes have been filled, too. Like any small business, we’ve weathered times of want, but it’s definitely been plenty more than it hasn’t. Which is nice. Same goes for Baker’s Dozen Dry Cleaners next door (13th One’s On Us!), and C–Bock’s Bar down on the corner.

I can see the façade from the intersection: purple-trimmed display windows Tetrised with merch; front door covered with fliers advertising comic book conventions, local DnD groups, and video game competitions; and the signage above it all—Scribbles ’N Bits Comic Books & Video Games. In case “Comic Books & Video Games” wasn’t already self-evident, the logo leans into the narrative: the word “Scribbles” calling to mind a stylized POW! or WHAM! and “Bits” rendered in a blocky 8-bit font that’d fit right in on the screen of an original Game Boy. I’m not gonna go in the front, though. The store’s still open for another twenty minutes or so, and I’d prefer not to see Emma at the moment (or any customers, for that matter). Plus, I usually park in the alley anyway.

Next to the back door there’s a smoker’s pole and a sooty old orange crate for when I’m too lazy or too cold to stand. I unlock the door and walk into the office, which, not to be cliché, would best be described as organized chaos. “Wayne Manor meets Arkham Asylum,” Carrie was fond of saying. God, she was clever. Speaking of asylums, as I pull the door closed, I’m greeted by the muffled sound of crunchy guitar and frenetic drums behind Billie Joe Armstrong’s unmistakable vocals: Am I just paranoid, or am I just stoned? Green Day’s Basket Case must be playing up front. Very funny, universe! (I’d rather be stoned, for the record.) I modded an old Sony CD player boombox a while back with a Bluetooth receiver and mounted it in the front of the store—it pretty much exclusively plays rock music from the ’80s and ’90s via a dedicated iPad. So the pool of candidates for my entrance music is small, but still. Feels a little on the nose.

The old industrial desk we shared sits stolidly in the middle of the room, surrounded by discrete stacks of comics, consoles, and other merchandise, along with a couple of obligatory filing cabinets and a green leather banker’s chair for guests. I hesitate to sit. This is the first time I’ve been back at the store since Carrie died—Emma’s been holding down the fort in the interim (more capably than most teenagers probably would’ve, I’d add)—so it’s kind of an emotional roll of the dice. But I’m surprised to find that taking a seat behind the desk has a distinctly clarifying effect on my psyche. Nicotine minus nicotine. I am here. And I am on a mission:

Find Jazzwood.

In a flash, it occurs to me that maybe Carrie didn’t die by suicide at all. Maybe she was murdered. By Jazzwood. My adrenal glands say yes—my heart’s set racing at the thought—but my head says don’t get ahead of yourself. We haven’t even established whether Jazzwood is a person or not. Weird thing is, the name activates like, a single neuron in some superdistant recess of my brain. Like I’ve heard it somewhere before. Or dreamed it. 

Or hallucinated it. 

Regardless, if there is a memory associated with Jazzwood buried in my subconscious, it’s not dislodging at the moment. Not sure that I’d trust it anyway. Hi, I’m Alex. Small business owner and shit-for-brains. 

The desktop has one of those oversized paper calendars, but it mostly serves as a lavish notepad-slash-canvas for idle doodles. I’ve used an online calendar since basically forever, but Carrie kept it old school, recording all the important stuff in a studiously maintained appointment book. Which just so happens to have also been left on the desk. Okay, deep breath. Maybe there’s something important insi—

Static. Here it comes…

“Zero point zero zero one nine six zero.”

On impulse, I decide to write this one down. I want to tell Dr. Morrow about them, and I feel like if I’ve kept a log it’ll come across as less… delusional. So I snatch a pen and scratch the latest number on the desk calendar. 0.001960. Turns out I can remember the one before this, too—I was a little spooked that it ended with nine-one-one. 0.000911. Then, above them, as a header of sorts, I write “Messages.” Because how else would I label them? 

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

title: Behind the scenes: Jazzwood, chapter 2
content:
Genre: Fiction
Note: For most of the last week, I was planning to write the final part of the Entropy and Restoration essay for this week’s post, but then I laid down to take a nap and had a burst of creativity/clarity about Jazzwood. So this week my practice of writing looks like taking and organizing my notes about chapter 2, which I share unedited below. For next week I will attempt to turn the notes into prose. Sorry if you were super into entropy.

  • Alex and Carrie co-own and run an independent video game store named Scribbles ’N Bits (abbreviated below as SNB)

    • Full name is Scribbles ’N Bits Comic Books & Video Games

    • Inspiration for location and general vibe: Bits + Pixels in Richmond, Virginia

      • (Contact Bits + Pixels to request photo / video walkthrough? And/or try to visit in person some day?)

    • Further research: https://www.wired.com/story/independent-video-game-stores/

    • What businesses are next-door? Maybe they come into play later on… Street in front of SNB is where Alex receives the business card later on in the story… Think Water St. in Decorah

  • In addition to running SNB, Alex streams part-time on Twitch, mostly in the evenings, and mostly speedrunning retro video games like Super Metroid

    • His Twitch handle is starl3xx (pronounced “star-lex”), which is derived from stylizing his name as ★LEX, which he used to do all the time as a kid, in notebooks (using the 4-color pens!), on Trapper Keepers, etc

      • Within the smaller community of SM speedrunners, he’s simply called “Lex” (like IRL how ShinyZeny is “Zeni” and Oatsngoats is “Oats”)

      • Not sure if this streaming aspect is introduced in chapter 2 or later… but important to set up for future plot points, including the chat box incident during one of starl3xx’s streams

Chapter outline:

  • Alex: “What I need is a place to think…”

    • (Chapter 1 ends w/“I need a cigarette.” so maybe rethink one/both lines)

    • Basic objective of the chapter is A) for Alex to start teasing out who/what Jazzwood is and B) introduce SNB (and the street it’s on) as major/main story location

  • Alex drives from Carrie’s apartment to “the store” (SNB)—without necessarily consciously deciding to; brain autopilot takes over… SNB office is a sanctuary of sorts for him. Find Jazzwood note in his pants pocket

  • Describe SNB

    • Located on old “Main Street” that has been subsumed by new growth in big suburb… Main Street has thus been rehabilitated and lifted up as town pride (kinda like downtown Grimes… Stillwater, MN also comes to mind, vibe-wise)

    • SNB logo has “Scribbles” written in comic book font and “Bits” written in 8-bit video game font

    • SNB was originally founded with investment from Alex & Carrie’s dad, has since become sustainable for Alex, Carrie, and 1-2 part-time employees

    • Growing up and into adulthood, Carrie was more into comics, and Alex video games; before Carrie’s death, they co-managed the day-to-day operations of SNB while serving as resident experts in their respective specialties

      • In future edit of ch. 1, maybe introduce some comic book memorabilia in Carrie’s apartment? Poster on the wall?

      • Maybe she was into Element Girl as a kind of macabre reverse-foreshadowing? (see Sandman #20)

  • Alex goes in the back door, which is accessed via an alley, so as not to be seen by SNB Employee, a reliable teenager (≈18/19… based on former student?) SNB has employed for a couple of years

  • There’s an old boombox CD player that Alex modded with a Bluetooth adapter to play 80s and (mostly) 90s music from a dedicated iPod as in-store music

    • Mention what song is playing when he walks in? If so (he can identify the song through the wall): Basket Case by Green Day? (“Very funny, universe.”)… Could have lots of fun w/’90s rock music throughout the story. Actually, maybe this is important plot point..? 😏

  • Alex considers where to begin w/Jazzwood

    • Alex first decides the note could only possibly be for him, based on where Carrie hid it… This is def. a message from Carrie to Alex

    • Why didn’t she provide any more info? Wait, maybe she did?

    • Is Jazzwood a who or a what? Or a where? And is Jazzwood related to Carrie’s death somehow? Was Carrie… murdered by Jazzwood? Getting ahead of himself

    • Also, nothing concrete comes to mind, but it definitely feels like he’s seen or heard “Jazzwood” before… subconscious recognition

    • Alex hears static crackling and then The Voice: “Zero point zero zero one nine six zero.”

  • SNB Employee walks into office to close SNB for the day, nearly interrupting The Voice and startling Alex (Employee: “I didn’t realize you were here…”)—Alex internally wonders whether they heard The Voice… maybe he apologizes for being gone a lot recently / thanks Employee for stepping up at the store; that awkward time when you know someone is grieving but you’re not sure what to say or do… Eventually Employee goes home

  • Alex orders food, smokes a cigarette out back, decides to begin by looking through Carrie’s files in the office for any mentions of Jazzwood

  • Alex decides to start transcribing what The Voice is saying, both for himself and Dr. Morrow… Grabs Carrie’s appointment book from desk, calls to mind the latest message (“0.001960.”) (Maybe he also remembers previous message from end of ch. 1 since it memorably ended w/9-1-1—0.001911), opens appointment book to write it down, discovers first clue

  • Knock on the door makes him nearly crap his pants

    • It’s just the food he ordered lolol

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

title: Entropy and Restoration, Part 3
content:
Genre: Nonfiction / Personal essay
Previously: Part 1, Part 2

Nine years ago, while I was receiving treatment for Hodgkin lymphoma, my oncologist wrote a prescription for liquid lidocaine. The chemo had wreaked havoc on the insides of my mouth and I was in considerable pain; I was instructed to gargle the lidocaine for 3-5 minutes to numb my mouth and (theoretically) provide some relief. Nine seconds in, I started gagging. I persisted about five more before spitting it into the kitchen sink.1 This is basically what it’s been like trying to wrap my head around entropy. Just when I think I’ve established a tenuous, abecedarian understanding of it, I come face-to-face with something like

…and whatever the neurological version of a gag reflex is kicks in. My brain simply refuses to process it.

I’m not mad about it, though, because in my anecdotal observation, about 95% of what’s published about entropy is in fact more specifically about how everyone (author/s excepted, of course) completely miscomprehends, perverts, and/or otherwise butchers the shit out of entropy’s true meaning. Take this peer-reviewed journal article, for example: Entropy and Information Theory: Uses and Misuses. I’ll wager a steak dinner you can’t make it through the entire thing without feeling stupid a single time, even if you already know what entropy is.

But it really does seem like there’s a shared, foundational belief among scientists that entropy is just completely misunderstood by pretty much all of humanity. As research for this essay I was recently corresponding with a retired professor of chemical physics, and at one point he offhandedly quipped, “Most American journalists use words they do not understand to express thoughts that have not been tested against reality.” Translation: You’re out of your depth here, buddy. Leave the physics to the physicists. (Thanks for suggesting that I’m a journalist, though!)

The video about entropy that I found most instructive is even set up as a takedown of sorts—it’s titled A better description of entropy (emphasis mine). So let’s go ahead and define what entropy isn’t. Entropy is not a measure of “chaos” or “disorder.” As far as I can tell, every joke ever made about entropy is based on this misconception:

This also at least partially explains my laughably bad graph from back in Part 1. Even if you (charitably) concede that the ??s on the y-axis represent “Order”—so that that as time progresses, order decreases—what you’d have is not only embarrassingly off-the-mark, but also an unintentionally prescient caricature of the erroneous way entropy is popularly described. I repeat: Entropy is not a measure or portrayal of disorder. The cosmos is not hurtling inexorably toward utter chaos, and neither is your living room, thank God.

What is it, then? Well, for starters, it’s not just a theory; it’s the second law of thermodynamics, forcryingoutloud. Yes, entropy “lives in the sphere of physics,” but guessing that it “primarily inhabits the smaller sphere of cosmology” (as I also did in Part 1) would be like saying that mayonnaise “lives in the sphere of condiments” but is primarily used in “the smaller sphere of potato salads”—it’s a major player in way more contexts than I’ve given it credit for. Anyway, here’s how one particle physicist describes it:

“What [the second law of thermodynamics] basically says is that physical processes tend to run down. Early in any physical process, energy is in a more organized, ‘usable’ form. As time goes on this energy gets degraded into a more disorganized, unusable form. In technical language, ‘entropy’ increases. A simple example is that a rolling ball will slow down, and eventually stop, as its energy of motion is worn down by friction and converted into heat.”2

And if you’re a simpleton like me, you may appreciate the uncomplicated definition provided in the video I mentioned earlier: Entropy is “a measure of how spread-out energy is.” And because entropy always increases (just trust me), energy is “always spreading out, going from a clumped-up state to a spread-out state.”

This is what’s behind the theory of the apocalyptic-sounding “heat death” of the universe, which posits that in the far (like, far) distant future “the universe would evolve to a state of no thermodynamic free energy and would therefore be unable to sustain processes that increase entropy.” Imagine all of the stars in the universe as ice cubes floating in an immense tank of water—eventually, all of the ice cubes will melt and the water’s temperature will return to a state of equilibrium. Clumped-up → spread-out. That’s entropy.

I think.

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

1

I know this because I timed it.

2

Barr, Stephen M. Modern Physics and Ancient Faith. Notre Dame, Ind: University of Notre Dame Press, 2003. 60.

title: Entropy and Restoration, Part 2 (Interpolation)
content:
Genre: Listicle with class

interpolation

noun \ in-ˌtər-pə-ˈlā-shən \ the insertion or addition of words into a text or a conversation

We have so much to talk about.

  • First let me say thank you again for not only subscribing but actually reading the things that’ve been showing up in your inbox. I’m really enjoying this, and I hope you are too. Kinda takes me back to my blogging days, but something about this feels, I don’t know… more grown-up? Furtive, somehow? Regardless, I’m truly appreciative of your literal and figurative investment.

  • Speaking of being appreciative, it has been such a delight hearing from you all in various ways—leaving comments on and/or liking posts, sending me text messages, etc. Honestly, it’s super encouraging. It feels like a mini community is being fostered here and I’m into it. If there’s someone you know who’d enjoy Neon Parentheses (my preemptive apology for the obligatory bit of self-promotion we both know is coming…), consider encouraging them to subscribe! (Ick. Post hoc apologies as well.)

  • So by now you’ve ascertained that I’m writing this update—er, interpolation—via bullet points, and there are a few reasons for this. First, I think it is probably right and salutary that I break the fourth wall and address you, the audience, directly every now and again. I wanted to let you all know, for example, that I recently pitched How Do You Give A Hamburger A Bath? to honest-to-God children’s book editors via a Twitter event called #PBPitch (Picture Book Pitch). No bites this time around, but there’s another one coming in October, and at this point, I’m just thrilled to actually have something to pitch!

    HOW DO YOU GIVE A HAMBURGER A BATH? is a step-by-step guide where you’ll learn allll the tips and tricks that will ensure the very *best* burger bathing experience. Things can fall apart pretty fast, so remember: DON’T GET THE BUNS WET! #PBPitch

    — Jake Bouma (@jakebouma)

  • Second, I am writing this now-multipart essay on the fly, and a lot has happened since I decided to start Entropy and Restoration last week. My original intent was for the piece to be a single, lengthy essay titled Entropy, Restoration, and Redemption with the same basic setup as Part 1 from last week, PLUS a cogent explanation of entropy after I’d gone and done the research, PLUS a brief explication about how entropy might (or might not?) relate to or even explain the superlatively satisfying nature of restoration videos PLUS the essay’s (imagined) pièce de résistance: a moving theological meditation on the nature of God as observed through the lens of entropy. But.

  • It turns out entropy is quite complicated. I’ve been dimples deep in it for a couple of weeks and am just now getting what I think is a basic handle on it and how I might write about it in a way that’s not only digestible, but that goes down easily enough that making the turn to theology doesn’t induce nausea. So I decided to break the essay up into smaller pieces for the time being.

  • Now, I am absolutely not the first person to think about entropy in relation to theology. Not only have entire books been written about the topic (theologians ruminating on entropy as well as scientists ruminating on entropy’s theological implications), but entire books been written about the history of literature written about it. But wait, there’s more. Entire books have been written about eras within the history of literature written about it. Behold: Entropic Creation: Religious Contexts of Thermodynamics and Cosmology, which “consider[s] the cultural and religious responses to the second law of thermodynamics, from around 1860 to 1920.” Amazon indicates it’s 278 (assuredly riveting) pages. So yeah, I quickly realized I’m just a smidge out of my depth here.

  • Which is why, on a lark, I decided to cold-email a physics professor who authored a helpful essay on entropy and theology I’d found. This led to one of the sickest burns I’ve ever been on the receiving end of:

    Let the record show that I am not, in fact, a serious person. Let the record also show that Professor Impatient and I had a lovely and illuminating 55-minute telephone conversation earlier today, and my notes from that phone call will help immensely as I craft Part 3 for next week. This guy was quite the character, though, letmetellya. I laughed out loud at this bit from one of the (many) emails he sent before our phone call, for example; he wrote: “Scientists have been obscuring [entropy’s] meaning ever since. (Theologians no longer believe in meaning.)” ZING!

  • Anyway, I’m working on something more substantive and non-interpolative for next week, but this has been fun. For me, at least. (I am not a serious person.)

  • Hey, while I’ve got you here—Here’s a fun example of the Baader–Meinhof phenomenon: I’ve been reading Dave Itzkoff’s biography of Robin Williams, so I recently sought out and listened to Reality… What A Concept, Robin’s first stand-up album from 1979. About 10 minutes in, he slips into an impersonation of Mr. Rogers which includes microwaving a hamster, and as Williams-as-Rogers explains:

    “...But I have to do that. Because you realize, boys and girls, that the universe is entropying [sic]. Can you say entropy? Nice try.”

    Maybe the last place I expected entropy to pop up, but there you have it. I like Williams but FWIW I wouldn’t recommend Reality… What A Concept; you’re welcome to form your own opinion via Music or Spotify or someplace else.

  • Finally, I was pretty proud of this one:

    Finally finished writing my book on chronology. It’s about time.

    — Jake Bouma (@jakebouma)

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

title: Entropy and Restoration, Part 1
content:
Genre: Nonfiction / Personal essay

I don’t fully understand what entropy is. And to be honest, I harbor more than a little bit of embarrassment at this ignominious gap in my general knowledge. I can tell you off the top of my head, for example, the difference between artificial intelligence (AI) and artificial general intelligence (AGI), that Proxima Centauri is the closest star to our own sun, and that methane in cow burps (not farts) accounts for a small percentage of anthropogenic climate change. But when it comes to entropy, I can’t really even offer a Wikipedia-entry’s-first-paragraph-level definition. That said, I’m pretty sure—like 99.99% confident—that the concept of entropy lives in the sphere of physics, and sorta sure—like 66.66% confident, maybe?—that it primarily inhabits the smaller sphere of cosmology. But again, [shoulder shrug].

It feels like entropy is a concept that I should by this point fully grok, and yet my offhand understanding of it amounts to something like this:

It’s bad, I know. And even though I’m 99.999% confident that my high school physics teacher isn’t reading this right now, I do feel like I should apologize. So: I’m sorry, Mr. Christianson. If it makes you feel any better (you = Mr. C.), I still think about my group’s robotics project from your class. You may remember our robot as the wheeled one that, when backing up, played an 8-bit rendition of the piano hook from Linkin Park’s “In the End.” Anyway, that was a long time ago. As Linkin Park reminds us in the very same song, The clock ticks life away, it's so unreal. Preach.

Back to entropy, though. My unfamiliarity with it means, among many other things, that I’m woefully unequipped to tactically deploy the exquisite adjectival construction, entropic. ￼Maybe someday. Regardless, my hope is that during the process of writing this two-part essay, entropy and I will become if not BFFs, then at least convivial acquaintances. Meantime, I’d like to continue the confessional vibe we’ve established and clue you in to another little facet of my life.

A few years ago, I fell down a strange and super-niche rabbit hole: Vintage tool restoration videos on YouTube.

I’m officially fall-asleep-on-the-recliner-while-watching-vintage-power-tool-restoration-videos-on-YouTube years old.

— Jake Bouma (@jakebouma)

I like to think that my YouTube content consumption is fairly diverse, representative in some ways of my varied interests IRL. My current subscriptions (206 as of this writing) include channels about music/musicians I’m into, video game speed running, lawn care, technology, comedy, space exploration, television and movies, &c. But I am a mega sucker for a well-made restoration video—and I’d like to introduce you to the absolute G.O.A.T., a guy who goes by the moniker my mechanics. Here’s a great example of a my mechanics upload:

That video has just under 25 million views. His channel has 2.3 million subscribers. Then there are the 700+ truly hardcore fans / sad sacks like me who support him financially on Patreon. Clearly there is a market for this content—but why?

My six-year-old son would say it’s because the videos are “satisfying.” Oh yeah—my wife and son have long since gotten into them as well. We refer to them as “fixing videos,” and have developed a bit of a routine around watching one as a family, which we do with (now that I’m writing this down) alarming frequency: We all melodramatically convey our disgust when the to-be-restored object is initially shown, clucking our tongues and declaring how “sick” and/or “totally roached” it is, then at the end, when the fully restored object is visually contrasted with the original, we alternate between the melodramatic disgust and a commensurately histrionic chorus of oohs and aahs for the shiny final product. Our performances aside, my son is absolutely right—these videos are super satisfying.

My hunch when I started thinking about writing about this was that maybe my fascination with restoration videos is in some way tied to entropy, even though my understanding of entropy is nebulous at best. And yet for some reason I feel like the very concept of restoration is in some way bound up in (direct? inverse?) relationship with the concept of entropy.

Maybe I’m way off. Maybe I’m onto something. Maybe you’re heaving expletives at your screen because the meaning of entropy is just so [bleep]ing obvious. Regardless, I’m going to do a bit of research in the next few days and then put together my conclusions for Part 2 for next week.

If you’re reading this, Mr. Christianson, don’t give up on me just yet.

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.

title: Jazzwood, chapter 1
content:
Genre: Fiction
Notes: Good morning from Lake Okoboji! This is a completed first draft of the first chapter of a story I’m calling Jazzwood. I’ve included the text from last week’s post below for clarity and continuity’s sake (and because I made a handful of super-slight alterations). Thanks for reading!

Snub out the cigarette. Drop the butt into the half empty bottle of Mountain Dew. Exhale. Grab the rail of the balcony and watch the traffic go by.

One thing at a time.

You can do this. There isn’t exactly a choice at this point, but a little positive self-talk never hurt anyone, right?

It’s been two weeks now, and it’s still hard to stomach: Carrie’s dead. Two weeks, and still zero clue why she of all people would commit suicide. Die by suicide, Dr. Morrow would correct me. Had corrected me. Right. Well either way, my sister’s dead. So.

Another cigarette. One more, then back inside the too-empty apartment.

Dr. Morrow. I haven’t yet told my therapist yet about the voices I’ve been hearing. Hallucinating? Hard to say. Google was inconclusive at best. Not connected with Carrie’s death, though—the voices started at least a month ago, and she wasn’t found until fifteen days ago. Found. Jesus. Take a drag.

Snub out the cigarette. Drop the butt into the half empty bottle of Mountain Dew. Exhale. Slide open the patio door and walk inside.

Carrie’s apartment is dim, lit only by the viscous afternoon light stretching through the slatted patio blinds. Prison-cell shadows slanted across the wall and carpet. Even though most of her stuff’s boxed up, her scent lingers, intimating lilac now and agai—

“Zero point zero zero one eight one seven.”

A voice. The voice. Distant and glitchy, as if my brain is short circuiting—which it almost certainly is. White noise, cryptic string of numbers, white noise, silence, repeat a few times. Literally maddening. On top of everything else.

“Zero point zero zero one eight six five.”

The numbers are going up, maybe. I haven’t been writing them down. A bit weird to transcribe a hallucination, feels like. Anyway, the voice definitely seems to be coming from somewhere external, even if it’s being created internally, in my mind. Still: untraceable. Simultaneously fixed and elusive, like the ubiquitous taunt of a smoke alarm chirp-chirping its low battery status. It appears to have stopped for now, though, thankfully. I’ll address that problem later, with Dr. Morrow. Right now there’s the matter of finishing Carrie’s paperwork and getting the hell out of this damned apartment.

“Damned” as in cursed.

Carrie and I were close—calling or texting each other daily, often several times a day—but the last time I was actually in her apartment was about a year and a half ago, right after dad died. So maybe not cursed, exactly, but you can see why I’d be anxious to leave this place in the rear view. At least dad’s death was normal, quote-unquote. Still, for all the clichés there are about death being The End, turns out that a fair amount of a person persists after their life, mostly in the form of paperwork. 

Speaking of, all’s that’s left is I just need to find a pen and scrawl my name on a few documents. The movers will take care of the rest—boxes and furniture and stuff. Going into storage. In pro baseball, teams will sometimes make trades with other teams that include a PTBNL: Player To Be Named Later. I’ve started thinking about everything headed for the storage unit as STBDWL: Stuff To Be Dealt With Later. We had an estate sale after dad died, but it doesn’t feel right getting rid of Carrie’s things just yet. I don’t know. I’m sure Dr. Morrow would want me to “process that some more” and “examine my grief.” For now: storage. STBDWL. 

A pen. Saw one on the kitchen counter earlier. Can’t believe Carrie still has it, to be honest. Had it. Ugh. It’s one of those old school clicker pens with four different ink colors—black, red, blue, and green. They were all the rage when I was in junior high, which is why Carrie and I hounded mom for weeks to special order us a box. Carrie’d found some deal in the back pages of one of dad’s Popular Mechanics where you could get a dozen with a custom message printed on them. Mom capitulated, and after some heated negotiation, we’d agreed on PROPERTY OF CARRIE AND/OR ALEX CALDWELL—take that, would-be pubescent pen thieves!—and as far as I know, this is the sole survivor of the original set. 

Except, it doesn’t even work. When I try to select blue to start signing documents, nothing clicks. And no ball point appears, either. Cheap plastic piece of shit. Even if it is sentimental. Shaking it accomplishes nothing, so I unscrew it and aha: no ink cartridges. Weird. Instead, there’s what, a piece of paper? Some sort of… hidden message?

Ohmygod. Suicide note. If she’d written one, nobody’d found it.

Yet.

If that’s what this is, it’s short. The piece of paper is tiny; a rolled-up sticky note, looks like. And at a glance the message is brief, but it’s unmistakably Carrie’s handwriting—there’s a sort of refined youthfulness to it, like teenagers in formalwear at prom. The message’s letters alternate black, red, blue, and green like the pen used to write them, presumably. It says:

FIND
JAZZWOOD

Not a suicide note, then. What the hell, Carrie?

Then, as if on cue, the voice: “Zero point zero zero one nine one one.”

I need a cigarette.

Thank you so much for subscribing! I’m grateful for each and every one of you, especially Neon Parentheses’s Founding Members: Mike G., Denise L., Stephen P., Ann T., and Michelle W.
